content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 12, 1991

Charles H. Bell
Nielsen, Merksamer, Hodgson,
Parrinello & Mueller
770 L Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request For Advice
Our File No. A-91-077

Dear Mr. Bell:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice which you
sought on behalf of the California Legislative Forum. Your
questions concern the gift limitations and the lobbying provisions
of the Political Reform Act.1/

Specifically, your questions concern the method to be used to
determine the source and amount of gifts to officials when the
gifts are paid for by multiple donors, and are made through the
California Legislative Forum (CLF). You asked about the
application of the gift limitations of Sections 89504 and 89505 to
a dinner provided by the California Legislative Forum to
California officials.

With the exceptions noted below, your letter accurately
reflects my advice. Under the facts you presented, the "sponsors"
(lobbyist employers and others who donate toward the event) of the
dinner provided by the California Legislative Forum (CLF) are the
"sources" of the gift. To determine the amount each sponsor paid
toward each official's dinner, the following method should be
appropriate:

1. Determine the pro rata fair market value of the dinner by
dividing the total cost of the dinner by the total number of
guests (including paying guests and non-paying guests).

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Divis ion 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Charles H. Bell
Page Two


     2.  Deduct from the payment made by the sponsor to the CLF
the fair market value of attendance by the sponsor's employees.

     3.  Divide the sponsor's payment less the amount paid for the
sponsor's own employees by the number of guests who attended free
of charge.  This will result in the amount the sponsor paid toward
each official's meal.  In your example, this amount is $32.

     The example provided on page 2 of your letter appears to
accurately reflect the method of determining the amount of each
sponsor's payment which applies toward the gift limits in Sections
89504 and 89505 for each official who received a gift.

     However, with regard to lobbyist employer reporting of
"activity expenses," the following method should be used to report
the gifts to officials in the situation you have described:

     1.  The "Name and Address of Payee" column should show
         the California Legislative Forum, as intermediary
         for the dinner to the officials.

     2.  The "Amount Benefitting Each Official" is $200.  A note
         should be added to indicate that the lobbyist employer's
         pro rata share of the gift is $32, which is the amount
         that applies toward the $250 gift limit for each official
         from the employer.

     3.  The "Total Amount of Activity" is $2,000.  A note should
         be added to indicate that this payment was a portion of
         the total cost of the event, provide the total cost of
         the event, and note that it was paid for by multiple
         donors.

     In addition, in the situation you described, the CLF would
not be considered a source of the gifts provided at the event.
Rather, because the CLF is merely receiving payments from the
"sponsors" to pay for the event at which officials will receive
gifts, the CLF is acting as an intermediary for the gifts.  To
ensure proper disclosure by each official who receives a gift at
the event, and by each lobbyist employer and other person who has
disclosure obligations under the Act, the CLF must keep accurate
records of receipts and payments in connection with the event.

Charles H. Bell
Page Three


     I hope this response adequately addresses the questions
presented in your letter. If you have any questions, please
contact me at (916) 322-5662.

                                   Sincerely,

                                   Scott Hallabrin
                                   Acting General Counsel

                                   [illegible signature]
                                   By:  Jeanne Pritchard
                                        Chief, Technical Assistance
                                        and Analysis Division
